meta.source: v174 fixed demo + v217 representative NGN item-format sampler extracted from canonical DB
meta.note: Small public preview database only. Full paid bank is not shipped through public demo routes.

notes.note: Patient resting comfortably, oriented to person, place, and time. Reports mild pain at surgical site, rated 3/10 on a 0-10 scale. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No skin lesions noted. Surgical dressing to right femur dry and intact.

Patient observed to be restless and confused, disoriented to time. Verbalized, 'difficulty catching my breath.' Respiratory rate 30 breaths/min, shallow. Auscultation revealed crackles in bilateral lung bases. Fine, non-blanching petechiae noted on chest and axillae. Skin pale and clammy. SpO2 88% on room air.

orders.text: Stat ABGs.
Stat Portable Chest X-Ray.
Continuous Pulse Oximetry monitoring.
Cardiac Monitor.
Neurological checks every 1 hour and PRN.
Initiate IV fluids: Normal Saline at 100 mL/hr.
Administer 100% O2 via non-rebreather mask.

hp.hpi: Patient is 24 hours post-operative for a comminuted right femur fracture. He was stable overnight but developed sudden onset of restlessness, confusion, and dyspnea this morning.
hp.pmh: No significant past medical history. Non-smoker. No known allergies.

social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a rural area, approximately 45 minutes from the nearest rehabilitation facility.
social_history.social_determinants_of_health: Limited access to public transportation, which may pose challenges for follow-up appointments and consistent rehabilitation post-discharge.

hp.impression: Suspected Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES) secondary to comminuted right femur fracture, presenting with respiratory distress, neurological impairment, and petechial rash.
hp.past_medical_history: No significant past medical history. Non-smoker. No known allergies.
hp.history_of_present_illness: Patient is 24 hours post-operative for a comminuted right femur fracture. He was stable overnight but developed sudden onset of restlessness, confusion, and dyspnea this morning.

physical_examination_findings.skin: Fine, non-blanching petechiae visible on chest and axillae. No rashes or lesions elsewhere.
physical_examination_findings.extremities: Right lower extremity in immobilizer, surgical dressing dry and intact. No signs of infection or excessive swelling at surgical site. Peripheral pulses palpable and strong bilaterally.
physical_examination_findings.respiratory: Tachypneic (RR 30 breaths/min), shallow respirations. Auscultation reveals crackles in bilateral lung bases. Reports 'difficulty catching my breath.'
physical_examination_findings.neurological: Disoriented to time, restless, confused. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation.
physical_examination_findings.cardiovascular: Tachycardic (HR 118 bpm). Blood pressure 102/68 mmHg. Capillary refill <3 seconds.
physical_examination_findings.general_appearance: Appears restless, confused, and anxious. Skin pale and clammy.

imaging.report: CHEST X-RAY (PORTABLE): Findings: Diffuse bilateral patchy infiltrates, most prominent in the peripheral lung fields. No evidence of pneumothorax or pleural effusion. Impression: Findings are consistent with acute lung injury, possibly related to fat embolism syndrome, though non-specific. Clinical correlation recommended.

questions.prompt: The 24-year-old male client in the Medical-Surgical Unit is being evaluated for Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES) post-fracture. Current chart cue: vital signs show rr 30, spo2 88% Select the action the nurse should implement now based on the documented cues. Link the priority condition, actions, and monitoring parameters.

structure.prompt: The 24-year-old male client in the Medical-Surgical Unit is being evaluated for Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES) post-fracture. Current chart cue: vital signs show rr 30, spo2 88% Select the action the nurse should implement now based on the documented cues. Link the priority condition, actions, and monitoring parameters.
structure.stem: The 24-year-old male client in the Medical-Surgical Unit is being evaluated for Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES) post-fracture. Current chart cue: vital signs show rr 30, spo2 88% Select the action the nurse should implement now based on the documented cues. Link the priority condition, actions, and monitoring parameters.

rationale.trap: Do not be trapped into prioritizing less urgent interventions like pain management or documentation when a client exhibits acute respiratory distress and hypoxemia, as these signs demand immediate physiological support.
rationale.golden_rule: Immediate recognition and intervention for life-threatening complications are paramount. Prioritize airway, breathing, and circulation.
rationale.core_concept: Clinical Judgment: Prioritization and Recognition of Complications.
rationale.answer_analysis:
The client's presentation of sudden onset dyspnea, tachypnea, tachycardia, confusion, hypoxemia (SpO2 88%), crackles, and petechiae on the chest and axillae 24 hours post-femur fracture is highly indicative of Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES). FES is a serious complication of long bone fractures, characterized by a triad of respiratory distress, neurological impairment, and petechial rash. The fat globules released from the bone marrow enter the bloodstream, travel to the lungs, and cause pulmonary inflammation, edema, and impaired gas exchange, leading to hypoxemia. They can also travel to the brain and skin.
The *first* priority for a client experiencing acute respiratory distress and hypoxemia is to improve oxygenation. Elevating the head of the bed facilitates lung expansion, and applying oxygen (e.g., high-flow nasal cannula or non-rebreather mask) directly addresses the hypoxemia. Subsequent actions would include notifying the healthcare provider, initiating continuous pulse oximetry, and preparing for potential intubation and mechanical ventilation if respiratory status deteriorates further. Administering analgesics (A) is not the priority in acute respiratory distress. Immediate surgical intervention (C) is not the primary treatment for FES; management is supportive. Documenting and monitoring (D) without intervention is inappropriate for a rapidly deteriorating client with life-threatening symptoms.

mnemonic.title: FES: The "RAPID" Signs
mnemonic.content:
R - Respiratory Distress (dyspnea, tachypnea, hypoxemia)
A - Altered Mental Status (confusion, restlessness, agitation)
P - Petechial Rash (non-blanching, on chest, axillae, conjunctiva)
I - Increased Heart Rate (tachycardia)
D - Decreased Oxygen Saturation (hypoxemia)

questions.client_needs: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies
questions.prompt: A 76 year old client admitted to the medical floor with pneumonia and suspected Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone (SIADH) is awaiting transfer to the ICU. The nurse is reviewing recent assessment findings.

structure.prompt: A 76 year old client admitted to the medical floor with pneumonia and suspected Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone (SIADH) is awaiting transfer to the ICU. The nurse is reviewing recent assessment findings.

rows.text: Client has dry mucous membranes and decreased skin turgor
Client oriented to person only, confused about place and time

rationale.golden_rule: The nurse must monitor for neurological changes and fluid overload, as severe hyponatremia can lead to seizures and cerebral edema.
rationale.answer_analysis: Hyponatremia (serum sodium <135 mEq/L) is the defining characteristic of SIADH due to excessive water retention. A level of 120 mEq/L is consistent with a diagnosis of SIADH, making it an 'Expected Finding' for this condition, though it signifies severe hyponatremia requiring immediate intervention. In SIADH, inappropriate secretion of ADH leads to increased water reabsorption in the kidneys, resulting in highly concentrated urine. An elevated urine specific gravity (normal 1.005-1.030) indicates concentrated urine and is an 'Expected Finding' for SIADH. Moderate headache and nausea are common neurological and gastrointestinal symptoms caused by cellular swelling and cerebral edema secondary to hyponatremia, which is a hallmark of SIADH. Therefore, these are 'Expected Findings' for a client with SIADH and low sodium levels. Dry mucous membranes and decreased skin turgor are clinical signs of dehydration and fluid volume deficit. SIADH is characterized by inappropriate water retention and fluid volume excess or euvolemia, not dehydration. Therefore, these findings are 'Unexpected Findings' and inconsistent with the pathophysiology of SIADH. Altered mental status, including confusion and disorientation, is a significant neurological manifestation of severe hyponatremia and cerebral edema in SIADH. This is an 'Expected Finding' for a client with SIADH and low sodium levels.

notes.note: Patient, 28-year-old female, G2P1 at 32 weeks gestation, admitted to Labor and Delivery. Reports regular uterine contractions occurring every 5 minutes, lasting approximately 40 seconds, for the past two hours. Expresses discomfort with contractions, rating pain 6/10 on a 0-10 scale. Past medical history includes well-controlled asthma.
Oriented patient to room and unit procedures. Initiated continuous fetal monitoring and uterine activity monitoring. Obtained vital signs. Reviewed patient's medical history.

Vital signs: Blood Pressure 128/78 mmHg, Heart Rate 92 bpm, Respiratory Rate 18 breaths/min, Temperature 37.0°C, Oxygen Saturation 98% on room air. Fetal heart rate baseline 142 bpm with moderate variability and accelerations present, no decelerations noted. Uterine contractions palpated every 5 minutes, lasting 40 seconds, moderate intensity. Cervical examination performed by physician: 3 cm dilated, 70% effaced, station -1.
Physician notified of assessment findings. Orders received for laboratory work and tocolytic medication. Patient educated regarding preterm labor management and anticipated medication administration.
Patient verbalized understanding of the plan of care and expressed desire to delay labor. Appears anxious but cooperative. *.....MI.Rn8244*

orders.text: Admit to L&D for preterm labor.
Bed rest with bathroom privileges.
IV Lactated Ringer's at 125 mL/hr.
Continuous fetal heart rate and uterine activity monitoring.
Routine lab work: CBC, UA, Chem Panel.
Magnesium Sulfate 4g IV loading dose over 30 minutes, followed by 2g/hour continuous infusion.
Monitor maternal vital signs, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, deep tendon reflexes (DTRs), and urine output every hour while on Magnesium Sulfate.
Obtain serum Magnesium level 4 hours after initiation and every 6 hours thereafter, or as clinically indicated.
Hold Magnesium Sulfate for respiratory rate <12/min, absence of DTRs, or urine output <30 mL/hr. Notify provider immediately.
Administer Betamethasone 12mg IM q24h x2 doses for fetal lung maturity.

hp.hpi: 28-year-old G2P1 at 32 weeks gestation presents to Labor & Delivery with regular uterine contractions occurring every 5 minutes, lasting 40 seconds, for the past 2 hours. Reports mild lower back pain and pelvic pressure. Fetal movement noted as usual.
hp.pmh: Well-controlled asthma, diagnosed in childhood. Uses albuterol PRN, last used 3 months ago. No recent exacerbations.

physical_examination.general: Alert, oriented, appears uncomfortable with contractions.
physical_examination.abdominal: Soft, gravid, fundal height consistent with 32 weeks gestation. Uterus firming with contractions.
physical_examination.pelvic_exam: Cervix 3 cm dilated, 70% effaced, station -1. Membranes intact.
physical_examination.respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Respiratory effort unlabored between contractions.
physical_examination.fetal_assessment: Fetal heart rate reassuring, baseline 140 bpm, moderate variability, accelerations present, no decelerations.

hp.social_history: Lives with partner. Works full-time as a retail associate. Reports occasional difficulty attending all scheduled prenatal appointments due to inflexible work hours and limited public transportation options. Non-smoker, occasional social alcohol use prior to pregnancy, abstinent since conception.

questions.prompt: The 28-year-old female client in the Pediatric Unit is being evaluated for Preterm Labor Management. Current chart cue: vital signs show temp 37.0°C, hr 92, rr 18, bp 128/78 Select the intervention that addresses the priority risk shown in the chart. Complete the sentence using the safest chart-supported choices.

structure.scenario: A 28 year old G2P1 at 32 weeks gestation presents to the labor and delivery unit with regular uterine contractions occurring every 5 minutes, lasting 40 seconds. Her cervical exam reveals 3 cm dilation and 70% effacement. Fetal heart rate is reassuring. Her medical history includes well-controlled asthma. The nurse reviews the patient's medical history and current presentation. Considering the goal of delaying labor, the nurse anticipates administering [BLANK] as the initial tocolytic agent.
structure.prompt: The 28-year-old female client in the Pediatric Unit is being evaluated for Preterm Labor Management. Current chart cue: vital signs show temp 37.0°C, hr 92, rr 18, bp 128/78 Select the intervention that addresses the priority risk shown in the chart. Complete the sentence using the safest chart-supported choices.
structure.stem: The 28-year-old female client in the Pediatric Unit is being evaluated for Preterm Labor Management. Current chart cue: vital signs show temp 37.0°C, hr 92, rr 18, bp 128/78 Select the intervention that addresses the priority risk shown in the chart. Complete the sentence using the safest chart-supported choices.

rationale.trap: A common error is selecting tocolytics with gestational age restrictions, such as indomethacin after 32 weeks, or those primarily indicated for neuroprotection rather than labor suppression.
rationale.answer_analysis: The safest priority is the keyed response. This response best addresses the current cue pattern: Preterm Labor Management. The correct answer is Nifedipine. **Nifedipine (Calcium Channel Blocker):** This is the most appropriate choice for this patient. Nifedipine relaxes smooth muscle, including the uterus, by blocking calcium entry. It is effective in delaying preterm labor and is a safe alternative when beta-agonists are contraindicated. It is particularly suitable…
rationale.educator_note: Wave 6 rewrite: stem and rationale were revised for chart-driven wording; answer key and options were not changed.

mnemonic.title: Tocolytic Contraindications
mnemonic.content:
Remember 'MINTS' for common tocolytics and key considerations:
**M**agnesium Sulfate: Monitor for toxicity (RR, DTRs, urine output). Contraindicated in myasthenia gravis.
**I**ndomethacin: Use <32 weeks. Contraindicated in renal/hepatic disease, asthma, NSAID sensitivity, peptic ulcer disease.
**N**ifedipine: Monitor for hypotension, headache, flushing. Contraindicated in significant cardiac disease, hypotension.
**T**erbutaline: Contraindicated in cardiac disease, diabetes, hyperthyroidism, and ASTHMA.
**S**afety: Always assess maternal and fetal well-being.

nexusWave6AuthenticityRewrite.originalPromptPreview: Complete the clinical judgment statement by selecting the best response.

notes.note: Patient, a 14-year-old female, arrived via emergency medical services (EMS) accompanied by parents. Parents report a 2-day history of increased thirst (polydipsia), frequent urination (polyuria), diffuse abdominal pain, and generalized lethargy. Parents also report a 3 kg weight loss over the past week. Patient is drowsy but rousable to verbal stimuli. Mucous membranes are observed to be dry. Breath noted to have a distinct fruity odor. Vital signs upon arrival: BP 90/50 mmHg, HR 120 bpm, RR 32 breaths/min (deep and labored), Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Point-of-care blood glucose 580 mg/dL. Urine dipstick positive for large ketones and large glucose.

Physician notified of patient's presentation and initial assessment findings. Orders received for rapid intravenous fluid resuscitation with 0.9% Normal Saline bolus, continuous cardiac monitoring, and stat laboratory blood work. Peripheral intravenous access established in the right antecubital fossa with a 20-gauge catheter.

Patient tolerating initiation of intravenous fluid resuscitation. Continuous monitoring of vital signs and cardiac rhythm in progress. Awaiting comprehensive laboratory results to guide further management. *.....AM.Rn2094*

orders.text: Calcium Gluconate 1 gram IV push over 5-10 min STAT, then PRN per protocol Cardiac membrane stabilization
Regular Insulin 10 units IV push STAT Shift potassium intracellularly
Dextrose 50% 50 mL IV push STAT, with insulin Prevent hypoglycemia
Sodium Polystyrene Sulfonate (Kayexalate) 15 grams PO Q6H until K+ normal Potassium excretion
Continuous Cardiac Monitoring Continuous Monitor for arrhythmias
Vital Signs Every 1 hour Monitor hemodynamic status

hp.chief_complaint: Increased thirst, frequent urination, abdominal pain, and lethargy for 2 days.
hp.hpi: Patient is a 14-year-old female presenting to the emergency department with a 2-day history of polyuria, polydipsia, diffuse abdominal pain, and generalized lethargy. Parents report a significant weight loss of 3 kg over the past week. No fever, vomiting, or diarrhea reported. Patient has a known history of Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, diagnosed 2 years prior, but has had recent challenges with medication adherence.

physical_examination.abdomen: Soft, non-distended, mild diffuse tenderness to palpation.
physical_examination.respiratory: Kussmaul respirations (deep and labored). Breath with a distinct fruity odor.
physical_examination.neurological: Alert and oriented x3 when stimulated, but lethargic.
physical_examination.general_appearance: Drowsy but rousable to verbal stimuli, appears acutely ill.

hp.social_history: Patient lives with both parents. Parents report recent job loss for the primary wage earner, leading to significant financial strain and difficulty affording insulin and blood glucose testing supplies, which has impacted the patient's ability to consistently manage her diabetes.

Patient has a known history of Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, diagnosed 2 years prior, but has had recent challenges with medication adherence.

questions.prompt: Review the clinical scenario. Highlight the cues that require priority follow-up.

structure.prompt: Review the clinical scenario. Highlight the cues that require priority follow-up.
structure.passage: A 14 year old female presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of increased thirst, frequent urination, abdominal pain, and lethargy. Her parents report she has lost 3 kg in the past week. On examination, she is drowsy but rousable, has dry mucous membranes, and her breath has a fruity odor. Vital signs are: BP 90/50 mmHg, HR 120 bpm, RR 32 breaths/min (deep and labored), Temp 37.0°C (98.6°F), SpO2 98% on room air. Point-of-care blood glucose is 580 mg/dL (32.2 mmol/L). Urine dipstick shows large ketones and glucose.

tokens.text: A 14 year old female presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of increased thirst, frequent urination, abdominal pain, and lethargy.
Her parents report she has lost 3 kg in the past week.
On examination, she is drowsy but rousable, has dry mucous membranes, and her breath has a fruity odor.
Vital signs are: BP 90/50 mmHg, HR 120 bpm, RR 32 breaths/min (deep and labored), Temp 37.0°C (98.6°F), SpO2 98% on room air.
Point-of-care blood glucose is 580 mg/dL (32.2 mmol/L).
Urine dipstick shows large ketones and glucose.

rationale.answer_analysis: The highlighted cues represent the most abnormal or time-sensitive findings in the scenario. Background history may matter, but priority highlighting should focus on deterioration, abnormal vital signs, acute neurologic/respiratory/circulatory changes, or safety threats.
rationale.golden_rule: Highlight cues that change risk now; do not highlight every background detail.
rationale.trap: Treating background history and acute deterioration cues as equally urgent.

mnemonic.title: DKA FAST
mnemonic.content:
D - Dehydration (Fluid first!)
K - Ketones (Insulin second!)
A - Acidosis (Monitor ABGs)
F - Fluid (IV fluids are key)
A - Airway (Assess breathing)
S - Sugar (Check glucose often)
T - Treat (Electrolytes, underlying cause)

questions.stem: Review the clinical scenario. Highlight the cues that require priority follow-up.

questions.client_needs: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential
questions.prompt: Mr. John Smith, a 82 year old male, reports sudden onset of palpitations, dizziness, and shortness of breath. His cardiac monitor shows an irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves and a rapid ventricular rate of 140-160 beats/minute.

structure.prompt: Mr. John Smith, a 82 year old male, reports sudden onset of palpitations, dizziness, and shortness of breath. His cardiac monitor shows an irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves and a rapid ventricular rate of 140-160 beats/minute.

options.text: Obtain a 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG).
Administer prescribed diltiazem intravenously.
Notify the healthcare provider of the patient's condition.
Assess the patient's vital signs, level of consciousness, and presence of chest pain.

rationale.trap: A common trap is to immediately notify the provider or administer medication before completing a thorough initial assessment and obtaining diagnostic data.
rationale.golden_rule: Always 'Assess before Act' to ensure patient safety and guide appropriate interventions, especially in dynamic cardiac situations.
rationale.core_concept: Immediate assessment and stabilization are paramount in patients presenting with new-onset cardiac dysrhythmias.
rationale.answer_analysis: The nurse must first assess the patient's stability, then obtain diagnostic confirmation, followed by notifying the provider, and finally implementing ordered interventions.

notes.note: Patient, 68-year-old male, admitted with generalized weakness and fatigue. Initial ECG revealed peaked T-waves, PR prolongation, and QRS widening. Serum potassium level 6.8 mEq/L. Blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute and irregular, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Patient reports missing last two hemodialysis sessions due to transportation issues.
Repeat ECG shows T-waves less peaked, PR interval shortening, and QRS complex narrowing. Heart rate 95 beats per minute and regular. Patient reports slight improvement in generalized weakness.

Repeat serum potassium level 5.1 mEq/L. Blood pressure 140/85 mmHg, heart rate 88 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. ECG shows resolution of peaked T-waves, normal PR interval, and normal QRS complex.

questions.prompt: The nurse is caring for a client admitted with severe hyperkalemia. After administering IV insulin and dextrose, and sodium polystyrene sulfonate, the nurse reviews the client's most recent laboratory results and vital signs. Which trend in the client's data *most strongly* indicates an effective therapeutic response to the hyperkalemia treatment and resolution of immediate life-threatening cardiac effects?

structure.prompt: The nurse is caring for a client admitted with severe hyperkalemia. After administering IV insulin and dextrose, and sodium polystyrene sulfonate, the nurse reviews the client's most recent laboratory results and vital signs. Which trend in the client's data *most strongly* indicates an effective therapeutic response to the hyperkalemia treatment and resolution of immediate life-threatening cardiac effects?

rows: Sinus rhythm with tall, peaked T waves
Sinus rhythm, T waves less peaked, PR prolonged
Sinus rhythm, T waves resolving, PR normal

trend_data.ECG Rhythm: Sinus rhythm with tall, peaked T waves
Sinus rhythm, T waves less peaked, PR prolonged
Sinus rhythm, T waves resolving, PR normal

options.text: Time: 08:00; Potassium (mEq/L): 7.2; ECG Rhythm: Sinus rhythm with tall, peaked T waves; HR: 58; BP: 108/68; Glucose (mg/dL): 110
Time: 10:00; Potassium (mEq/L): 6.5; ECG Rhythm: Sinus rhythm, T waves less peaked, PR prolonged; HR: 64; BP: 116/72; Glucose (mg/dL): 98
Time: 12:00; Potassium (mEq/L): 5.8; ECG Rhythm: Sinus rhythm, T waves resolving, PR normal; HR: 72; BP: 124/78; Glucose (mg/dL): 105
Time: 14:00; Potassium (mEq/L): 4.5; ECG Rhythm: Normal Sinus Rhythm; HR: 78; BP: 128/82; Glucose (mg/dL): 112

rationale.trap: Do not solely focus on the serum potassium level as the primary indicator of immediate life-threatening cardiac resolution; prioritize ECG changes which directly reflect myocardial electrical stability.
rationale.answer_analysis: An effective therapeutic response to severe hyperkalemia treatment is primarily indicated by a decreasing trend in serum potassium levels towards the normal range (3.5-5.0 mEq/L) and, critically, the resolution of life-threatening cardiac manifestations. Calcium gluconate works rapidly to stabilize the myocardial cell membrane, reducing the risk of dysrhythmias, but does not lower serum potassium. Insulin with dextrose shifts potassium from the extracellular to the intracellular space, causing a rapid but temporary decrease in serum potassium. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) works slower by exchanging sodium for potassium in the gastrointestinal tract, leading to potassium excretion. Therefore, the most crucial indicators of effective treatment are a sustained decrease in serum potassium levels coupled with normalization of the ECG (e.g., resolution of peaked T-waves, return to regular rhythm, absence of PR prolongation or QRS widening) and improvement in cardiac rate and rhythm. While other symptoms like muscle weakness may improve, the immediate priority in hyperkalemia management is to prevent fatal cardiac dysrhythmias. A sustained decrease in potassium and a stable cardiac rhythm confirm the effectiveness of the interventions in mitigating acute risks. Continued monitoring is essential as the effects of some treatments are temporary, and the underlying cause (ESRD) requires definitive management like hemodialysis.

mnemonic.title: HyperK Treatment: C BIG K DROP
mnemonic.content:
C - Calcium Gluconate (Cardioprotection)
B - Bicarbonate / Beta-agonists (Shift K+ intracellularly)
I - Insulin (Shifts K+ intracellularly)
G - Glucose (Prevents hypoglycemia with insulin)
K - Kayexalate (Sodium Polystyrene Sulfonate - Eliminates K+)
D - Diuretics (Loop diuretics - Eliminates K+)
R - Renal Dialysis (Definitive elimination)
O - Observe/Monitor (Continuous assessment)
P - Potassium Restriction (Dietary management)

questions.prompt: Mr. Henry, a 37 year old male, is admitted to the medical-surgical unit from the ED with profound weakness and shortness of breath. His lab results show hemoglobin 5.8 g/dL and hematocrit 17.5%, with a recent history of melena. He is pale, tachycardic at 118 bpm, and reports dizziness upon standing.

structure.prompt: Mr. Henry, a 37 year old male, is admitted to the medical-surgical unit from the ED with profound weakness and shortness of breath. His lab results show hemoglobin 5.8 g/dL and hematocrit 17.5%, with a recent history of melena. He is pale, tachycardic at 118 bpm, and reports dizziness upon standing.

rows.text: Initiate a peripheral IV line with an 18-gauge catheter.
Administer an oral iron supplement immediately.
Encourage ambulation to the bathroom with minimal assistance.
Place client on continuous pulse oximetry and apply oxygen via nasal cannula.
Obtain a blood sample for type and crossmatch.

rationale.golden_rule: Priority is to assess for signs of hypovolemic shock and ensure circulatory stability by establishing IV access and preparing for fluid resuscitation.
rationale.answer_analysis: Mr. Henry presents with severe anemia (Hgb 5.8 g/dL), symptoms of hypoxemia (shortness of breath, tachycardia, pallor), and orthostatic symptoms (dizziness upon standing). The history of melena suggests active gastrointestinal bleeding. * **Initiate a peripheral IV line with an 18-gauge catheter.** * **Indicated**: An 18-gauge (or larger) IV is essential for rapid administration of blood products (e.g., packed red blood cells) which are highly likely to be needed with a hemoglobin of 5.8 g/dL and active bleeding. It also allows for fluid resuscitation and medication administration. * **Administer an oral iron supplement immediately.** * **Non-Essential**: While iron deficiency is a common cause of anemia, oral iron supplements have a slow onset of action and will not acutely improve Mr. Henry's critical hemoglobin level or severe symptoms. Acute management of severe anemia focuses on transfusion and identifying/stopping the source of bleeding. Oral iron can be considered for long-term management once the acute crisis is addressed. * **Encourage ambulation to the bathroom with minimal assistance.** * **Contraindicated**: With severe anemia, tachycardia, shortness of breath, and reported dizziness upon standing, Mr. Henry is at extremely high risk for falls, syncope, and exacerbation of cardiac strain due to reduced oxygen-carrying capacity. Activity should be severely restricted, and assistance should be maximal (e.g., bedpan, commode at bedside with full assistance) to ensure safety and conserve energy. * **Place client on continuous pulse oximetry and apply oxygen via nasal cannula.** * **Indicated**: Severe anemia significantly reduces the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity, leading to tissue hypoxia. Supplemental oxygen helps compensate for the reduced oxygen delivery, alleviating symptoms like shortness of breath and tachycardia. Continuous pulse oximetry monitors oxygen saturation and the effectiveness of oxygen therapy. * **Obtain a blood sample for type and crossmatch.** * **Indicated**: With a hemoglobin of 5.8 g/dL and evidence of acute blood loss (melena), a blood transfusion is highly anticipated and often clinically indicated. Type and crossmatch is a prerequisite for administering blood products, ensuring compatibility and preparing for potential transfusion.

items.caseStem: Arthur Jenkins, an 82-year-old male, is brought to the Emergency Department by his daughter. He was recently discharged from a skilled nursing facility (SNF) two days ago after a fall. His daughter reports, 'He's been more confused and dizzy since he came home, and I noticed his hands shaking more than usual.' She states he takes 'a lot of pills' for his heart, blood pressure, and 'to help him relax.' She provides a discharge medication list from the SNF. Vital signs: BP 108/62 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 18 breaths/min, Temp 36.8°C (98.2°F), SpO2 96% on room air. Physical assessment reveals a fine tremor in both hands, unsteady gait, and disorientation to time and place. Capillary refill is <3 seconds, skin turgor is elastic. Urine output has been adequate according to his daughter.
items.stem: Highlight the findings that are most concerning for Arthur's current condition.
items.prompt: Highlight the findings that are most concerning for Arthur's current condition.
items.source: Gemini 2.5 Flash AI generator v24.3 advanced 100

structure.prompt: Highlight the findings that are most concerning for Arthur's current condition.

tokens.text: recently discharged from a skilled nursing facility (SNF) two days ago after a fall

rationale.core_concept: Medication reconciliation, polypharmacy, adverse drug reactions, geriatric care
rationale.answer_analysis: The patient's new onset of confusion, dizziness, increased hand tremor, disorientation, and unsteady gait are acute changes that are concerning. The daughter's report of him taking 'a lot of pills' suggests polypharmacy, which is a common cause of adverse drug reactions and cognitive changes in older adults. While his age and recent fall are important background information, the *new* symptoms are the most critical cues to recognize. His vital signs and hydration status appear stable, making them less concerning as primary causes for his acute neurological changes.
rationale.golden_rule: Separate relevant abnormal cues from background noise.
rationale.trap: Selecting normal findings as priority cues.

mnemonic.title: Case Study Rule
mnemonic.content: Carry forward earlier cues; later questions add data but keep the same client story.

items.clinical_focus: Medication reconciliation, polypharmacy, adverse drug reactions, geriatric care
items.client_needs: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies, Reduction of Risk Potential, Safety and Infection Control
items.caseStem: The nurse reviews Arthur's discharge medication list from the SNF, which includes: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Furosemide 20 mg daily, Metoprolol 25 mg BID, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily, Alprazolam 0.5 mg TID PRN anxiety, and Acetaminophen 650 mg PRN pain. His daughter also mentions he takes an over-the-counter (OTC) sleep aid, Diphenhydramine, 'most nights.'
items.stem: For each finding, indicate whether it supports or does not support the potential causes of Arthur's current symptoms.
items.prompt: For each finding, indicate whether it supports or does not support the potential causes of Arthur's current symptoms.
items.source: Gemini 2.5 Flash AI generator v24.3 advanced 100

structure.prompt: For each finding, indicate whether it supports or does not support the potential causes of Arthur's current symptoms.

rationale.core_concept: Medication reconciliation, polypharmacy, adverse drug reactions, geriatric care
rationale.answer_analysis: Confusion, disorientation, dizziness, unsteady gait, and new tremors are all common manifestations of adverse drug reactions, especially in older adults with polypharmacy. The combination of Alprazolam (a benzodiazepine) and Diphenhydramine (an antihistamine with anticholinergic and sedative effects) significantly increases the risk of CNS depression, confusion, and falls in the elderly. While dehydration can cause confusion and dizziness, Arthur's stable vital signs and adequate urine output make it less likely to be the primary cause. An underlying neurological condition is possible but less likely to manifest acutely with these specific symptoms in the context of recent medication changes and polypharmacy. Therefore, all listed findings strongly support polypharmacy/adverse drug reaction.
rationale.golden_rule: Cluster cues into the most likely clinical meaning.
rationale.trap: Naming a diagnosis without connecting it to the cues.

mnemonic.title: Case Study Rule
mnemonic.content: Carry forward earlier cues; later questions add data but keep the same client story.

rationale.core_concept: Medication reconciliation, polypharmacy, adverse drug reactions, geriatric care
rationale.answer_analysis: Given Arthur's age, multiple medications (including CNS depressants like Alprazolam and Diphenhydramine), and the acute onset of confusion, dizziness, and tremor shortly after discharge with a complex medication regimen, an adverse drug reaction due to polypharmacy is the most likely primary cause. While acute infection, stroke, or electrolyte imbalance could cause similar symptoms, there are no specific cues (e.g., fever, focal neurological deficits, severe vomiting/diarrhea) to strongly support these as the primary cause at this time. The medication profile and symptom presentation align most closely with polypharmacy-related adverse effects.
rationale.golden_rule: Address the most urgent threat to life, safety, or deterioration first.
rationale.trap: Choosing the most familiar problem instead of the most urgent one.

items.stem: Which nursing interventions should be included in the plan of care for Arthur to address medication safety and reconciliation? Select all that apply.
items.prompt: Which nursing interventions should be included in the plan of care for Arthur to address medication safety and reconciliation? Select all that apply.

structure.prompt: Which nursing interventions should be included in the plan of care for Arthur to address medication safety and reconciliation? Select all that apply.

options.text: Obtain a complete and accurate list of ALL medications, including OTCs, supplements, and herbal remedies, from the patient and daughter.
Administer all prescribed medications as ordered to maintain therapeutic levels.
Collaborate with the healthcare provider and pharmacist to review the medication regimen for potential interactions, duplications, or unnecessary medications.
Educate Arthur and his daughter on the purpose, dosage, side effects, and administration of each medication.
Assess for barriers to medication adherence, such as financial constraints, cognitive impairment, or complex regimens.

rationale.core_concept: Medication reconciliation, polypharmacy, adverse drug reactions, geriatric care
rationale.answer_analysis: For a patient with suspected polypharmacy and adverse drug reactions, a comprehensive medication reconciliation is crucial. This includes obtaining a complete medication list (O1), collaborating with the healthcare team to optimize the regimen (O3), providing thorough education (O4), and identifying adherence barriers (O5). Administering all prescribed medications as ordered (O2) without a thorough review could exacerbate the problem if some medications are contributing to the adverse reaction or are no longer appropriate. The goal is to ensure safe and effective medication use, not just adherence to a potentially problematic regimen.
rationale.golden_rule: Choose interventions that directly address the priority hypothesis.
rationale.trap: Picking generic comfort measures before stabilizing the client.

mnemonic.title: Case Study Rule
mnemonic.content: Carry forward earlier cues; later questions add data but keep the same client story.

structure.prompt: Place the following nursing actions in the correct order to ensure safe medication reconciliation and patient education.

options.text: Review the reconciled medication list with Arthur and his daughter, explaining any changes and new instructions.
Compare the current home medication list (including OTCs) with the SNF discharge list and current hospital orders, identifying discrepancies.
Collaborate with the pharmacist and healthcare provider to resolve identified discrepancies and optimize the medication regimen.
Document the complete and reconciled medication list, patient education, and any remaining concerns.

rationale.core_concept: Medication reconciliation, polypharmacy, adverse drug reactions, geriatric care
rationale.answer_analysis: The correct order for medication reconciliation involves first gathering all medication information and identifying discrepancies (B). Next, the nurse collaborates with the healthcare team to resolve these discrepancies and make necessary adjustments (C). Once the regimen is optimized, the nurse educates the patient and family on the updated plan (A). Finally, all actions, education, and the finalized medication list are documented (D).
rationale.golden_rule: Take the safest, most immediate nursing action within scope.
rationale.trap: Delaying escalation when deterioration is present.

mnemonic.title: Case Study Rule
mnemonic.content: Carry forward earlier cues; later questions add data but keep the same client story.

structure.prompt: Based on the trend data, what is the most appropriate interpretation of Arthur's condition?

options.text: Arthur's symptoms are worsening, requiring further immediate intervention.
Arthur's condition is improving due to the medication adjustments.
Arthur is experiencing a new adverse drug reaction to the adjusted regimen.
Arthur requires a comprehensive neurological workup to determine the cause of his persistent symptoms.

rationale.core_concept: Medication reconciliation, polypharmacy, adverse drug reactions, geriatric care
rationale.answer_analysis: The trend data shows a clear improvement in Arthur's condition. His Confusion Assessment Method (CAM) score has transitioned from positive to negative, indicating resolution of delirium. His dizziness rating has significantly decreased from 8 to 2, and his tremor severity has reduced from 4 to 1. This positive trend directly correlates with the medication adjustments made to address polypharmacy and adverse drug reactions, indicating that the interventions were effective. There is no evidence of worsening symptoms or new adverse reactions, and with the significant improvement, a comprehensive neurological workup is not immediately indicated for persistent symptoms.
rationale.golden_rule: Evaluate whether objective client data show improvement or worsening.
rationale.trap: Evaluating by subjective improvement alone instead of objective outcomes.

timeline.text: Arthur Jenkins, an 82-year-old male, is brought to the Emergency Department by his daughter. He was recently discharged from a skilled nursing facility (SNF) two days ago after a fall. His daughter reports, 'He's been more confused and dizzy since he came home, and I noticed his hands shaking more than usual.' She states he takes 'a lot of pills' for his heart, blood pressure, and 'to help him relax.' She provides a discharge medication list from the SNF. Vital signs: BP 108/62 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 18 breaths/min, Temp 36.8°C (98.2°F), SpO2 96% on room air. Physical assessment reveals a fine tremor in both hands, unsteady gait, and disorientation to time and place. Capillary refill is <3 seconds, skin turgor is elastic. Urine output has been adequate according to his daughter.

The nurse reviews Arthur's discharge medication list from the SNF, which includes: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Furosemide 20 mg daily, Metoprolol 25 mg BID, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily, Alprazolam 0.5 mg TID PRN anxiety, and Acetaminophen 650 mg PRN pain. His daughter also mentions he takes an over-the-counter (OTC) sleep aid, Diphenhydramine, 'most nights.'
Based on the assessment findings and medication review, the nurse considers several potential causes for Arthur's acute changes.
The nurse has prioritized an adverse drug reaction due to polypharmacy as the most likely cause. The healthcare provider has been notified.
The healthcare provider has ordered a comprehensive medication reconciliation and review. The pharmacist has been consulted.
After the medication regimen was adjusted (Alprazolam discontinued, Diphenhydramine use discouraged, Tamsulosin dose reduced), Arthur's condition is reassessed over the next 24 hours.

cases.source: Gemini 2.5 Flash AI generator v24.3 advanced 100
